  "plan": "# Fix live console noise and favicon 404\n\n## What is actually broken\n- The only clearly site-controlled error in your list is &#96;/favicon.ico 404&#96;.\n- Errors from &#96;inject.bundle.js&#96;, &#96;runtime.lastError&#96;, and New Relic &#96;ERR_BLOCKED_BY_CLIENT&#96; are almost certainly from a browser extension/ad blocker context, not your deployed app code.\n\n## Targeted implementation plan\n- Verify favicon handling in the WordPress theme and add explicit support where missing in [&#96;/Users/katespurr/Halifax Now/hfx-now/wp-content/themes/halifax-now-broadsheet/functions.php&#96;](/Users/katespurr/Halifax%20Now/hfx-now/wp-content/themes/halifax-now-broadsheet/functions.php).\n- Add a fallback favicon link in [&#96;/Users/katespurr/Halifax Now/hfx-now/wp-content/themes/halifax-now-broadsheet/header.php&#96;](/Users/katespurr/Halifax%20Now/hfx-now/wp-content/themes/halifax-now-broadsheet/header.php) so browsers always have a deterministic icon URL.\n- Add actual favicon asset files under the theme assets directory (new files) and point fallback URLs there.\n- If &#96;/favicon.ico&#96; is still required by crawlers/browsers, add a server-level or WP-level route/redirect to the theme icon path so the root request no longer returns 404.\n\n## Validation checklist\n- Open live site in an incognito window with extensions disabled: confirm favicon 404 is gone.\n- Re-test with normal profile: confirm any remaining &#96;inject.bundle.js&#96; / New Relic blocked logs are extension-side only.\n- Confirm page source contains either WordPress Site Icon tags and/or fallback icon tags.\n\n## Notes\n- Current codebase search found no New Relic integration strings and no favicon assets tracked in this repo, which supports the extension/adblock diagnosis for the non-favicon errors.",

```bash
git diff -- "wp-content/themes/halifax-now-broadsheet/functions.php" "wp-content/themes/halifax-now-broadsheet/header.php" "wp-content/themes/halifax-now-broadsheet/assets/favicon/favicon.svg"
```

```
diff --git a/wp-content/themes/halifax-now-broadsheet/functions.php b/wp-content/themes/halifax-now-broadsheet/functions.php
index 82cc0bf9..a3c01fb5 100644
--- a/wp-content/themes/halifax-now-broadsheet/functions.php
+++ b/wp-content/themes/halifax-now-broadsheet/functions.php
@@ -15,6 +15,7 @@ if (!defined('ABSPATH')) {
 function hfx_broadsheet_setup() {
 	add_theme_support('title-tag');
 	add_theme_support('post-thumbnails');
+	add_theme_support('site-icon');
 	add_theme_support(
 		'html5',
 		array(
@@ -94,6 +95,37 @@ function hfx_broadsheet_enqueue_assets() {
 }
 add_action('wp_enqueue_scripts', 'hfx_broadsheet_enqueue_assets');
 
+/**
+ * Print favicon tags when Site Icon isn't configured in WordPress.
+ */
+function hfx_broadsheet_favicon_fallback_tags() {
+	if ( function_exists( 'has_site_icon' ) &amp;&amp; has_site_icon() ) {
+		return;
+	}
+	$favicon_url = get_template_directory_uri() . '/assets/favicon/favicon.svg';
+	?&gt;
+	&lt;link rel="icon" href="&lt;?php echo esc_url( $favicon_url ); ?&gt;" type="image/svg+xml"&gt;
+	&lt;link rel="shortcut icon" href="&lt;?php echo esc_url( $favicon_url ); ?&gt;" type="image/svg+xml"&gt;
+	&lt;?php
+}
+add_action( 'wp_head', 'hfx_broadsheet_favicon_fallback_tags', 1 );
+
+/**
+ * Redirect root favicon requests to theme fallback icon.
+ */
+function hfx_broadsheet_handle_root_favicon_request() {
+	$request_path = isset( $_SERVER['REQUEST_URI'] ) ? (string) wp_unslash( $_SERVER['REQUEST_URI'] ) : '';
+	$request_path = (string) parse_url( $request_path, PHP_URL_PATH );
+
+	if ( '/favicon.ico' !== $request_path ) {
+		return;
+	}
+
+	wp_safe_redirect( get_template_directory_uri() . '/assets/favicon/favicon.svg', 301 );
+	exit;
+}
+add_action( 'template_redirect', 'hfx_broadsheet_handle_root_favicon_request', -1 );
+
 /**
  * Render browse template when slug is requested but WP page mapping is missing.
  *
diff --git a/wp-content/themes/halifax-now-broadsheet/header.php b/wp-content/themes/halifax-now-broadsheet/header.php
index f32cff4c..af97a0e5 100644
--- a/wp-content/themes/halifax-now-broadsheet/header.php
+++ b/wp-content/themes/halifax-now-broadsheet/header.php
@@ -9,6 +9,9 @@
 &lt;head&gt;
 	&lt;meta charset="&lt;?php bloginfo('charset'); ?&gt;"&gt;
 	&lt;meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1"&gt;
+	&lt;?php if ( ! function_exists( 'has_site_icon' ) || ! has_site_icon() ) : ?&gt;
+		&lt;link rel="icon" href="&lt;?php echo esc_url( get_template_directory_uri() . '/assets/favicon/favicon.svg' ); ?&gt;" type="image/svg+xml"&gt;
+	&lt;?php endif; ?&gt;
 	&lt;?php wp_head(); ?&gt;
 &lt;/head&gt;
 &lt;body &lt;?php body_class(); ?&gt;&gt;

```

_**Agent (default)**_

Implemented the plan end-to-end in the theme code, and all to-dos are now completed.

What I changed:

- Added WordPress site icon support in `wp-content/themes/halifax-now-broadsheet/functions.php` via `add_theme_support('site-icon')`.
- Added root favicon request handling in `wp-content/themes/halifax-now-broadsheet/functions.php`:
  - intercepts `/favicon.ico`
  - redirects to theme favicon asset (`/assets/favicon/favicon.svg`) with `301`
- Added fallback favicon tag in `wp-content/themes/halifax-now-broadsheet/header.php` when no WP Site Icon is configured.
- Added new favicon asset at `wp-content/themes/halifax-now-broadsheet/assets/favicon/favicon.svg`.

Validation performed:

- Checked edited files for lint issues: none found.
- Checked current live site response for `https://halifax-now.ca/favicon.ico` — it is still `404` right now.
  - This is expected until these changes are deployed to production.
- The other errors you pasted (`inject.bundle.js`, `runtime.lastError`, `ERR_BLOCKED_BY_CLIENT`, New Relic fetch failures) remain extension/ad-blocker side noise, not app code regressions.

Next step:

- Deploy these theme changes, then re-check console on live.
- After deploy, `favicon.ico` should stop 404ing, and remaining extension-generated console entries can be ignored or verified in an incognito window with extensions disabled.

_**Agent (default)**_

That warning is usually harmless and not from your theme code.

What I verified:

- Your repo does not set `Permissions-Policy` or `browsing-topics` anywhere.
- Live `https://halifax-now.ca` response headers currently do **not** include a `Permissions-Policy` header.

So the warning is likely coming from one of:
- a proxy/CDN/security layer in front of origin,
- a plugin/server config not in this repo,
- or a third-party request (sometimes extension-injected context).

How to confirm source quickly:

- In DevTools Network, click the exact request that logs the warning.
- Check **Response Headers** for `Permissions-Policy`.
- If it includes `browsing-topics`, that response is the source.

Fix:

- Remove `browsing-topics` from the header value.
- Use a conservative policy like:
  - `Permissions-Policy: geolocation=(), microphone=(), camera=()`
- If set in nginx/apache/CDN/WAF/plugin, update it there (not in theme PHP).

If you want, I can help you trace the exact layer by checking your server/CDN config path-by-path.

_**Agent (default)**_

That error means the commit itself didn’t fail due to your code — it failed because Git tried to open Cursor as the commit-message editor, and Cursor’s git editor helper got a `401` auth error.

## What happened
- Git ran `git commit` without `-m`, so it needed an editor.
- Editor configured was Cursor’s git editor shim: `git-editor.sh`.
- That editor process returned `401`, so Git aborted and asked for `-m`/`-F`.

## Quick fix right now
Run commit with an inline message (bypasses editor entirely):

```bash
git add .gitignore
git commit -m "chore: ignore local tool config directories"
```

## If you want editor-based commits to work again
- Re-authenticate in Cursor (sign out/in), then retry.
- Or switch Git editor to something local/stable (e.g. VS Code, nano).
- Or keep using `git commit -m` as your default flow.

If you want, I can give you the exact command to set a different editor temporarily vs permanently.

_**Agent (default)**_

Git is stopping because your local branch and `origin/main` both have unique commits, and Git needs an explicit strategy (merge vs rebase vs ff-only).

Use this one-off command (no config changes) to proceed cleanly:

```bash
git pull --rebase origin main
```

Then push:

```bash
git push origin HEAD:main
```

If you prefer merge commits instead, use:

```bash
git pull --no-rebase origin main
```

For your current situation (small local commit), `--rebase` is usually the cleanest.

_**User**_

is there a reson I ran into that issue?  I have been working with claude code and here 

---

_**Agent (default)**_

Yes — very normal when switching between tools/sessions.

Most likely causes in your case:

- You had **divergent history**: your local `main` had a new commit while `origin/main` also had new commits.
- Newer Git requires explicit pull strategy when branches diverge (`merge` vs `rebase`), so `git pull` failed until you specified one.
- During rebase, both histories touched `.gitignore`, creating the **conflict**.
- You also hit the commit editor error earlier because Cursor’s Git editor helper returned `401`, so the editor step failed unless `-m` was used.

Working across Claude Code + Cursor doesn’t break Git, but it increases chances of:
- commits being made in different moments/contexts,
- rebasing/pulling without a configured default,
- and editor/auth integration glitches.

If you want to avoid this next time, easiest habit is:
- always `git pull --rebase origin main` before committing/pushing,
- and commit with `-m` if editor integrations are flaky.
